title: Get started with tabularray
text: tblr() initializes data frame table object. Printing (calling tblr_as_latex()) produces LaTeX: Numeric columns right-aligned, text columns left-aligned. Column labels default column names.
code: df <- tibble(   continent = c("Europe", "Asia", "Asia"),   country   = c("Germany", "China", "Afghanistan"),   value     = c(0.17, 0.23, 11.3) )  df |> tblr() |> show_latex() \begin{center}     \begin{booktabs}{         colspec = {llr}         }         \toprule         continent & country     & value \\          \midrule         Europe    & Germany     & 0.17  \\          Asia      & China       & 0.23  \\          Asia      & Afghanistan & 11.30 \\          \bottomrule     \end{booktabs} \end{center}

title: Get started with tabularray
text: tabularray try formatting toolkit. currency, percentages, thousands separators, significant digits, custom date formats, per-column formatting, format column calling tblr() — example scales, base::format(), base::formatC(), base::sprintf(): formatted price now text column, literal $ contains escaped \$ — write plain character want let tblr handle escaping. Columns leave untouched converted sensible defaults: numbers get two decimal places (integer whole-valued columns without), dates, logicals, types rendered R display .
code: tibble(   item  = c("Widget", "Gadget"),   price = c(1999.5, 12.4),   added = as.Date(c("2024-01-15", "2024-11-03")) ) |>   mutate(     price = scales::dollar(price),     # currency, via scales     added = format(added, "%B %d, %Y") # a custom date format, via base R   ) |>   tblr() |>   show_latex() \begin{center}     \begin{booktabs}{         colspec = {lll}         }         \toprule         item   & price      & added             \\          \midrule         Widget & \$1,999.50 & January 15, 2024  \\          Gadget & \$12.40    & November 03, 2024 \\          \bottomrule     \end{booktabs} \end{center}

title: Get started with tabularray
text: Characters special LaTeX (&, %, $, _, #, {, }, ~, ^, \) escaped automatically text columns, ordinary data safe: Two things keep mind: Convert column types tblr(). Escaping decided column’s type table rendered. format column upstream (price example ), treated text escaped like text column. Labels, spanners, alignment, notes, options raw LaTeX. Values pass set_column_labels(), set_column_spanner(), set_colspec(), set_source_notes(), set_interface(), set_options() inserted verbatim, can use LaTeX commands — must escape special characters (e.g. write R\&D, R&D).
code: tibble(account = c("R&D", "50% off", "a_b")) |>   tblr() |>   show_latex() \begin{center}     \begin{booktabs}{         colspec = {l}         }         \toprule         account \\          \midrule         R\&D \\          50\% off \\          a\_b \\          \bottomrule     \end{booktabs} \end{center} df |>   tblr() |>   set_column_labels(value = "\\textbf{Value}") |>   show_latex() \begin{center}     \begin{booktabs}{         colspec = {llr}         }         \toprule         continent & country     & \textbf{Value} \\          \midrule         Europe    & Germany     & 0.17           \\          Asia      & China       & 0.23           \\          Asia      & Afghanistan & 11.30          \\          \bottomrule     \end{booktabs} \end{center}

title: Get started with tabularray
text: Group rows dplyr::group_by() tblr(), add spanning headers set_column_spanner() (call per spanner row, bottom-), tune appearance set_theme(): type argument selects LaTeX environment: "simple" (default, inline), "float" (numbered, captioned, supports notes), "break" (page-breaking).
code: starwars |>   filter(homeworld == "Tatooine", !name %in% c("Anakin Skywalker", "Darth Vader")) |>   select(name, height, mass, sex, birth_year) |>   mutate(sex = stringr::str_to_title(sex)) |>   group_by(sex) |>   tblr(type = "float", caption = "Tatooine characters") |>   set_column_labels(name = "", birth_year = "Birth Year") |>   set_column_spanner(c(height, mass) ~ "Physical") |>   set_theme(row_group_style = "panel") |>   show_latex() \begin{center}     \begin{talltabs}[         caption = {Tatooine characters}         ]{         colspec = {lrrr}         }         \toprule                            & \SetCell[c=2]{c} Physical        &      &            \\          \cmidrule{2-3}                            & height                           & mass & Birth Year \\          \midrule                            & \SetCell[c=3]{c} \textit{Male}   &      &            \\          \cmidrule{2-4}         Luke Skywalker     & 172                              & 77   & 19         \\          Owen Lars          & 178                              & 120  & 52         \\          Biggs Darklighter  & 183                              & 84   & 24         \\          Cliegg Lars        & 183                              & NA   & 82         \\          \addlinespace                            & \SetCell[c=3]{c} \textit{None}   &      &            \\          \cmidrule{2-4}         C-3PO              & 167                              & 75   & 112        \\          R5-D4              & 97                               & 32   & NA         \\          \addlinespace                            & \SetCell[c=3]{c} \textit{Female} &      &            \\          \cmidrule{2-4}         Beru Whitesun Lars & 165                              & 75   & 47         \\          Shmi Skywalker     & 163                              & NA   & 72         \\          \bottomrule     \end{talltabs} \end{center}

title: Get started with tabularray
text: generated markup needs tabularray package booktabs library. LaTeX preamble: reasonably recent tabularray (2023 later) recommended. Quarto R Markdown, output embedded verbatim — see README YAML setup. can also copy LaTeX editor.

title: Typeset LaTeX Tables using Tabularray
text: package lets typeset R objects dataframes LaTeX using tabularray. tabularray LaTeX package developed Jianrui Lyu provides modern unified alternative established space table-generating packages LaTeX. implementation R inspired R package gt. means construct table iteratively, adding formatting chaining functions together. Unlike gt, however, package focuses LaTeX output. intends maximize access functionality (LaTeX) tabularray package offering convenience functions common formatting tasks.

title: Typeset LaTeX Tables using Tabularray
text: can install development version tabularray GitHub :

title: Typeset LaTeX Tables using Tabularray
text: example , format dataset twice demonstrate tabularray works box fine-tuning applied.
code: library(dplyr) library(tabularray)  df <- starwars |>   filter(homeworld == "Tatooine", !name %in% c("Anakin Skywalker", "Darth Vader")) |>   select(name, height, mass, sex, birth_year) |>   arrange(desc(birth_year))  df[1, 1] <- "C$PO"

title: Typeset LaTeX Tables using Tabularray
code: df |>    mutate(sex = stringr::str_to_title(sex)) |>    group_by(sex) |>   tblr(type = "float", caption = "Starwars Creatures from Tatooine") |>    set_source_notes(     Note = "Entry C3PO altered to test characters that have a special meaning in LaTeX.",     Source = "R package \\texttt{dplyr}"   ) |>    set_colspec(height:birth_year ~ "X[r]") |>   set_column_labels(     name = "",     height = "Height",     mass = "Mass",     birth_year = "Birth Year"   ) |>    set_theme(row_group_style = "panel") |>    set_interface(width = "0.7\\linewidth") |>    set_column_spanner(     c(height, mass) ~ "Group 1",     birth_year ~ "Group 2"   ) |>    set_column_spanner(!name ~ "All my vars")

what: Usage with Quarto and R Markdown
title: Typeset LaTeX Tables using Tabularray
text: tabularray package produces LaTeX code can copy paste favorite LaTeX editor. However, designed shine combination literate programming. Integration seamless: just make sure load necessary LaTeX packages document. Quarto document’s YAML metadata, please include tabularray_packages.sty file contain dependencies listed : need modify chunk options. knitr automatically embed LaTeX markup verbatim.

title: Typeset LaTeX Tables using Tabularray
text: Among popular table packages, gt, kableExtra, flextable don’t target tabularray LaTeX package. tinytable — capable, actively developed, multi-format alternative (LaTeX, HTML, Typst, Word) many projects better choice. package deliberately narrower: tidyverse- gt-inspired interface built tabularray. leans two things. First, opinionated defaults layouts common academic publishing — row-group “panel” styling, column spanners, natural stub column. Second, direct per-column access raw tabularray code: column specifications (e.g. siunitx S X[r] columns), labels, spanners, notes, inner/outer specifications accept raw strings.

title: Print a tblr table object — print.tblr
Print a tblr table object — print.tblr
text: x tblr table object. ... additional parameters.

title: Print a tblr table object — print.tblr
text: character string containing LaTeX markup.

title: Set column specifications — set_colspec
text: Set column specification (colspec) one columns. column specification controls alignment , generally, tabularray column type. default, columns left-aligned ("l"), right-aligned ("r") numeric.

title: Set column specifications — set_colspec
text: x tblr table object. ... Expressions assignment column specifications. Expressions can named arguments form <column name> = <colspec> make use tidy-select. using tidy-select, expressions two-sided formulas (.e., form <LHS> ~ <RHS>). case, left-hand side used identify selections columns right-hand side specify column specification. Note column specifications supply assumed raw LaTeX strings; characters special meaning LaTeX escaped.

title: Set column specifications — set_colspec
text: tblr table object.

title: Set column specifications — set_colspec
text: Column specifications can just left ("l"), center ("c"), right ("r"). tabularray, column types derived column type Q. Useful shorthands include X (evenly distribute columns given fixed table width) S (format columns using siunitx, e.g. "S[table-format=2.3]").

title: Set column specifications — set_colspec
code: library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: ‘dplyr’ #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:stats’: #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from ‘package:base’: #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union df <- tibble(   continent = c("Europe", "Asia", "Asia", "Europe", "Asia"),   country = c("Germany","China", "Afghanistan","France","Taiwan"),   value = c(0.17, 0.23, 11.3, 17, 2.4) )  tblr(df) |>   set_colspec(value = "X", starts_with("c") ~ "c") #> \begin{center} #>     \begin{booktabs}{ #>         colspec = {ccX} #>         } #>         \toprule #>         continent & country     & value \\  #>         \midrule #>         Europe    & Germany     & 0.17  \\  #>         Asia      & China       & 0.23  \\  #>         Asia      & Afghanistan & 11.30 \\  #>         Europe    & France      & 17.00 \\  #>         Asia      & Taiwan      & 2.40  \\  #>         \bottomrule #>     \end{booktabs} #> \end{center}

title: Set column labels — set_column_labels
text: Define column labels (headers). default, column labels derived column names underlying dataframe. However, unlike column names, column labels unique. Note column labels used rendering table; functions still use original column names refer columns underlying dataframe.

title: Set column labels — set_column_labels
text: x tblr table object. ... Expressions assignment column labels. Expressions can named arguments form <column name> = <label> make use tidy-select. using tidy-select, expressions two-sided formulas (.e., form <LHS> ~ <RHS>). case, left-hand side used identify selections columns right-hand side specify column label. Note column labels specify assumed raw LaTeX strings; characters special meaning LaTeX escaped.

title: Set column labels — set_column_labels
text: tblr table object.

title: Set column labels — set_column_labels
code: library(dplyr) df <- tibble(   continent = c("Europe", "Asia", "Asia", "Europe", "Asia"),   country = c("Germany","China", "Afghanistan","France","Taiwan"),   value = c(0.17, 0.23, 11.3, 17, 2.4) )  tblr(df) |>   set_column_labels(value = "\\textbf{Value}", ends_with("y") ~ "COUNTRY") #> \begin{center} #>     \begin{booktabs}{ #>         colspec = {llr} #>         } #>         \toprule #>         continent & COUNTRY     & \textbf{Value} \\  #>         \midrule #>         Europe    & Germany     & 0.17           \\  #>         Asia      & China       & 0.23           \\  #>         Asia      & Afghanistan & 11.30          \\  #>         Europe    & France      & 17.00          \\  #>         Asia      & Taiwan      & 2.40           \\  #>         \bottomrule #>     \end{booktabs} #> \end{center}

title: Set column spanners — set_column_spanner
text: Set column spanner labels. Column spanner labels labels appear column labels (headers) span several . Call function spanner row table, starting one bottom.

title: Set column spanners — set_column_spanner
text: x tblr table object. ... Expressions assignment column spanner labels. Expressions two-sided formulas (.e., form <LHS> ~ <RHS>). left-hand side used identify selections columns via tidy-select right-hand side specify column spanner label. Note column spanner labels provide assumed raw LaTeX strings; characters special meaning LaTeX escaped.

title: Set column spanners — set_column_spanner
text: tblr table object.

title: Set column spanners — set_column_spanner
code: library(dplyr) df <- tibble(   continent = c("Europe", "Asia", "Asia", "Europe", "Asia"),   country = c("Germany","China", "Afghanistan","France","Taiwan"),   value = c(0.17, 0.23, 11.3, 17, 2.4) )  tblr(df) |>   set_column_spanner(!value ~ "Region") #> \begin{center} #>     \begin{booktabs}{ #>         colspec = {llr} #>         } #>         \toprule #>         \SetCell[c=2]{c} Region &             &       \\  #>         \cmidrule{1-2} #>         continent               & country     & value \\  #>         \midrule #>         Europe                  & Germany     & 0.17  \\  #>         Asia                    & China       & 0.23  \\  #>         Asia                    & Afghanistan & 11.30 \\  #>         Europe                  & France      & 17.00 \\  #>         Asia                    & Taiwan      & 2.40  \\  #>         \bottomrule #>     \end{booktabs} #> \end{center}

title: Set table interface — set_interface
text: function gives low-level access mandatory argument tblr environment, also called "inner specifications". LaTeX, section primarily used define column specification. tabularray LaTeX package provides -called "new interface" extends functionality mandatory argument allows targeting individual rows, columns, cells.

title: Set table interface — set_interface
text: x tblr table object. ... Named arguments spliced key-value pairs within mandatory argument tblr environment.

title: Set table interface — set_interface
text: tblr table object.

title: Set table interface — set_interface
text: mandatory argument list (mostly) key-value pairs. Use named arguments specify function. names values provide assumed raw LaTeX strings; characters special meaning LaTeX escaped. set key without value (e.g., "hlines"), pass character(0) example . Note value can vector one element. element automatically wrapped curly braces. Shortcut functions targeting specific parts interface set_colspec() take precedence specifications make .

title: Set table interface — set_interface
code: # Produce the following specification in LaTeX: # { #   hlines, #   vlines = {1, 3, 5}{dashed}, #   rows = {7mm} # }  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ x |>   set_interface(     hlines = character(0),     vlines = c("1,3,5", "dashed"),     rows = "7mm"   ) } # }

title: Set table options — set_options
text: function gives low-level access optional argument tblr environment, also called "outer specifications". example, types "float" "break" optional argument can contain caption, label, notes.

title: Set table options — set_options
text: x tblr table object. ... Named arguments spliced key-value pairs within mandatory argument tblr environment.

title: Set table options — set_options
text: tblr table object.

title: Set table options — set_options
text: optional argument list key-value pairs. Use named arguments specify function. names values provide assumed raw LaTeX strings; characters special meaning LaTeX escaped. value automatically wrapped curly braces. Shortcut functions targeting specific options caption specify call tblr() take precedence specifications make .

title: Add source notes — set_source_notes
text: Add table notes table. tblr table objects type "float" "break" support table notes.

title: Add source notes — set_source_notes
text: x tblr table object type "float" "break". ... Named arguments add notes table. Names provide label note; argument value actual note .

title: Add source notes — set_source_notes
text: tblr table object.

title: Add source notes — set_source_notes
code: library(dplyr) df <- tibble(   continent = c("Europe", "Asia", "Asia", "Europe", "Asia"),   country = c("Germany","China", "Afghanistan","France","Taiwan"),   value = c(0.17, 0.23, 11.3, 17, 2.4) )  x <- tblr(df, type = "float", caption = "A table with notes")  x |> set_source_notes(   Source = "Built-in R dataset",   Note = paste("This dataset comes with every R installation and is",                "available without explicitly loading it.") ) #> \begin{center} #>     \begin{talltabs}[ #>         caption = {A table with notes}, #>         remark{Source} = {Built-in R dataset}, #>         remark{Note} = {This dataset comes with every R installation and is available without explicitly loading it.} #>         ]{ #>         colspec = {llr} #>         } #>         \toprule #>         continent & country     & value \\  #>         \midrule #>         Europe    & Germany     & 0.17  \\  #>         Asia      & China       & 0.23  \\  #>         Asia      & Afghanistan & 11.30 \\  #>         Europe    & France      & 17.00 \\  #>         Asia      & Taiwan      & 2.40  \\  #>         \bottomrule #>     \end{talltabs} #> \end{center}

title: Set theme options — set_theme
text: function allows fine-tune way tblr table object rendered LaTeX.

title: Set theme options — set_theme
code: set_theme(   x,   table_indent = NULL,   table_booktabs = NULL,   row_group_style = NULL,   row_group_sep = NULL,   row_group_indent = NULL,   row_group_head = NULL,   row_group_head_alignment = NULL,   row_group_head_fontstyle = NULL,   row_group_head_cmidrule = NULL,   row_group_head_skip_stub = NULL )

title: Set theme options — set_theme
text: x tblr table object. table_indent Slightly indent table contents? TRUE default. FALSE removes indentation adding "@{}" sides column specification LaTeX. table_booktabs Format table using booktabs? TRUE default. booktabs LaTeX package modifies width (thickness) rules spacing around . row_group_style preset style row groups, either "left", "center", "separators", "panel". presets affect row_group_* options can overwritten. row_group_sep separate row groups? Default \\addlinespace. Another common choice \\midrule.  Specify character(0) remove separators. row_group_indent Indent rows group heading? TRUE default. Indentation achieved adding invisible column left blank grouped rows. row_group_head headings row groups? TRUE default. row_group_head_alignment value alignment (column specification) row group heads, "l" default. row_group_head_fontstyle font style use row group heads, "\\textit" (italic) default. options include: "\\textbf": boldface "\\textup": standard font style "\\textsc": small caps Note single command can used. row_group_head_cmidrule Add rule row group head? FALSE default. row_group_head_skip_stub stub (.e., first column) skipped aligning row group heads drawing rules underneath ? FALSE default.

title: Set theme options — set_theme
text: tblr table object.

title: Set theme options — set_theme
code: library(dplyr)  df <- tibble(   continent = c("Europe", "Asia", "Asia", "Europe", "Asia"),   country = c("Germany","China", "Afghanistan","France","Taiwan"),   gdp = c(0.17, 0.23, 11.3, 17, 2.4),   population = c(11.0, 7.3, 123.11, 5, 33) )  # apply preset "panel" but overwrite to left-align df |>   group_by(continent) |>   tblr() |>   set_theme(     row_group_style = "panel",     row_group_head_alignment = "l"   ) #> \begin{center} #>     \begin{booktabs}{ #>         colspec = {lrr} #>         } #>         \toprule #>         country     & gdp                              & population \\  #>         \midrule #>                     & \SetCell[c=2]{l} \textit{Europe} &            \\  #>         \cmidrule{2-3} #>         Germany     & 0.17                             & 11.00      \\  #>         France      & 17.00                            & 5.00       \\  #>         \addlinespace #>                     & \SetCell[c=2]{l} \textit{Asia}   &            \\  #>         \cmidrule{2-3} #>         China       & 0.23                             & 7.30       \\  #>         Afghanistan & 11.30                            & 123.11     \\  #>         Taiwan      & 2.40                             & 33.00      \\  #>         \bottomrule #>     \end{booktabs} #> \end{center}

what: Create a tblr table object — tblr
title: Create a tblr table object — tblr
text: function can used initialize dataframe table object class tblr. Usually, just starting point: Use pipe chain together functions incrementally add styling additional parts (notes) table.

title: Create a tblr table object — tblr
Create a tblr table object — tblr
text: df tibble data.frame typeset table. type type LaTeX table environment. can either "simple" (default), "float", "break". "simple" produces unnumbered untitled table intended simple tabular matter appear run text (see CMOS 3.51). "float" produces floating numbered table accommodates title table notes, similar threeparttable LaTeX. "break" produces numbered table can broken across pages instead floating. accommodates title table notes, similar longtable LaTeX. caption Title table. Needs specified unless type "simple". LaTeX output, title appears "caption" keyword.

title: Create a tblr table object — tblr
text: data.frame class tblr.

text: tblr() preserves dataframe called upon can continue use functions operate dataframes. function adds class tblr object saves metadata table (column types) attributes. metadata determines table rendered end workflow. modify columns call tblr(), risk breaking link. example, characters special meaning LaTeX escaped columns originally text-like (character strings factors). apply formatting function convert numeric column character vector call tblr() rendering, required take care escaping special characters .

title: Output a tblr object as LaTeX — tblr_as_latex
tabularray 0.0.0.9000
text: Numbers now formatted fixed number decimal places (2 default) instead significant figures. Values 3.04 longer truncated "3", formatting longer depends values column. Integer whole-valued columns render without decimals; dates types pass faithfully. Per-column currency/percent/significant- digit formatting remains user’s job, done tblr() packages scales gt. Column spanners longer error recent versions dplyr (whose stricter if_else() rejected array passed internally).

title: tabularray 0.0.0.9000
text: set_alignment() renamed set_colspec(), better reflects sets full tabularray column specification (alignment, also column types X siunitx’s S). Whether column’s contents escaped now determined column’s type render time rather recorded tblr() called. Columns formatted tblr() now escaped correctly. set_column_spanner() now errors spanner targets non-adjacent columns, previously rendered spanner wrong columns. Functions emit informative errors (e.g. caption missing, one grouping variable supplied, colspec set via set_interface()), warn set_colspec() / set_column_labels() given column name exist.

title: tabularray 0.0.0.9000
text: Removed dependency gt; LaTeX escaping now built . knitr longer required generate LaTeX output. remains suggested dependency seamless R Markdown / Quarto integration. Declared dependency R (>= 4.1.0), package already required (native pipe \(x) lambda syntax).
